reasons: All three agents (codex, claudecode, openclaw) produced the required gentle_rise.txt file with proper format and headers
All three agents correctly implemented linear regression using numpy.polyfit or scipy.stats.linregress
All three agents encountered the same data access limitation (DNS resolution failures for akshare endpoints)

Codex stands out for providing the most comprehensive documentation: it created screening_metadata.json with detailed data attempt logs, library availability checks, and explicit notes about the revision addressing review feedback
Codex's revise_gentle_rise.py is the most production-ready: clean structure, proper error handling, explicit documentation of the 300/301 prefix expansion and 11-point window requirement
Codex explicitly documented addressing both review issues: (1) expanding ChiNext universe from 300-only to 300/301 prefixes, (2) clarifying that 10 daily returns require 11 closing price points
OpenClaw's implementation is solid and comprehensive (169 lines with detailed logic), but lacks the metadata documentation that makes Codex's solution more auditable
ClaudeCode's implementation is well-structured but provides less documentation about the revision process and data access attempts
All agents produced functionally equivalent no-result outputs due to environment constraints, so the differentiator is code quality, documentation, and explicit response to review feedback
merge_notes: No merge needed. All three agents produced valid implementations with the same outcome (no qualifying stocks found due to data access limitations). Codex's solution is preferred for its superior documentation and explicit tracking of the revision process, making it the most maintainable and auditable implementation. The screening_metadata.json file provides valuable forensic information about what was attempted and why it failed, which is critical for debugging and future improvements.
